_comment: SolarQuest question bank. Levels are played in array order below (see levels.json order). Each level has an id, display name, emoji icon, and a `questions` array. Question `type` must be one of: mc | fill | match | flashcard. Keep the type sequence rotating (no repeats back to back) when you add more questions -- the game engine also enforces this automatically at runtime. See README.md for the full field reference per type.

levels.name: Life Cycle of a Star

questions.question: What is a large cloud of gas and dust in space called?
The Sun is currently a _____ sequence star.

questions.prompt: Match each word to its meaning.

pairs.match: A cloud of gas and dust
The earliest stage of a star
Leftover core after a red giant
Explosion of a dying massive star

questions.front: What happens when a main sequence star runs out of hydrogen fuel?
questions.back: It expands into a red giant (or a red supergiant if it's very massive)!

questions.question: What do we call the explosion of a dying massive star?
The most massive stars can collapse into a _____ _____ after they die.

questions.prompt: Match each word to its meaning.

pairs.match: Stars the size of the Sun swell into this
Leftover core of a supernova
Gravity so strong not even light escapes
The cold, dead remains of a white dwarf

questions.front: What are stars mostly made of?
questions.back: Hydrogen and helium gas!

questions.question: What is the name of the gas cloud that forms when a red giant's outer layers drift into space?
Stars are pulled together by _____, which increases pressure and causes protostars to heat up.

questions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.match: What a star much more massive than the Sun becomes
The heaviest element normal fusion inside a star can make
Only created during a supernova explosion
Hydrogen nuclei joining together to form helium, releasing energy

questions.front: Why does a white dwarf end up smaller than the red giant it came from?
questions.back: Because the red giant's outer layers drift away into space as a planetary nebula, leaving behind just the small, hot core!

questions.question: What does a star emit due to the nuclear fusion happening in its core?
The earliest stage of a star's life, when a contracting cloud starts to heat up and glow, is called a _____.

questions.prompt: Match each stage to what happens.

pairs.match: Gravity and pressure make the collapsing cloud heat up and glow
Hydrogen fuses into helium in a stable, balanced star
A Sun-sized star swells and cools as it runs low on hydrogen
The small, hot leftover core after a red giant's outer layers drift away

questions.front: What determines which path a star's life cycle takes -- red giant or red supergiant?
questions.back: Its size! Stars about the size of the Sun become red giants, while much bigger stars become red supergiants.

questions.question: Which is true about the Sun?
options: It is a main sequence star

questions.question: The larger a star is, the _____ and brighter it will be.

questions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.match: The explosion of a dying red supergiant
A very dense core left behind after most supernovae
What causes a red supergiant's core to explode
Does NOT occur once a star becomes a neutron star

questions.front: Why do bigger stars move through the main sequence stage faster than smaller stars?
questions.back: Because bigger stars burn through their hydrogen fuel faster -- the hotter and brighter they are, the quicker they use it up!

questions.question: What happens to a red giant's outer layers at the end of its life?
options: They're expelled into space as a planetary nebula
They turn into a black hole

questions.question: A white dwarf eventually cools and dims until it becomes a _____ dwarf, and finally a black dwarf.
Which of these is the correct order for a star about the size of the Sun?
options: Nebula, protostar, main sequence, red giant, white dwarf, black dwarf
Protostar, nebula, white dwarf, red giant
Main sequence, nebula, protostar, black hole

questions.question: Fusion of nuclei up to the size of _____ happens inside a red supergiant before it runs out of fuel.

questions.prompt: Match each object to how it forms.

pairs.match: Gas cloud formed when a red giant's outer layers drift into space
Leftover hot core after a low or medium mass star's outer layers escape
Forms when most supernovae collapse
Forms when the largest supernovae collapse

questions.front: Iron has atomic number 26, and copper has atomic number 29. What does that tell us about how each one formed?
questions.back: Iron (26) could have been made by normal fusion inside a star, but copper (29) is heavier than iron, so it could only have been made in a supernova!

questions.question: What must happen before a cloud of gas and dust can become a protostar?
options: Gravity pulls it together, increasing pressure and heat
It must lose all its hydrogen

questions.question: Once a star has 'turned on' through nuclear fusion, it is known as a _____ _____ star.

questions.prompt: Match each star type to its fate.

pairs.term: Stars about the size of the Sun
pairs.match: Become a red giant, then a white dwarf

pairs.term: Stars much more massive than the Sun
pairs.match: Become a red supergiant, then explode in a supernova
Collapse into a neutron star
Collapse into a black hole

questions.front: True or false: fusion still happens inside a neutron star.
questions.back: False! Fusion does not occur once a star has collapsed into a neutron star -- that stage is long over.

funFacts: Stars are made mostly of hydrogen and helium gas.
The Sun is a main sequence star -- the most stable stage of a star's life.
All naturally occurring elements up to iron are made by fusion inside stars.
Elements heavier than iron, like copper, can only be made in a supernova explosion.
A neutron star packs an incredible amount of mass into a tiny, super-dense core.
Once a star becomes a black hole, gravity is so strong that not even light can escape.
The bigger a star is, the faster it burns through its hydrogen fuel.
A white dwarf's leftover energy is squeezed into a tiny area, making it extremely hot.

levels.name: Basic Structure of an Atom

questions.question: What are the tiny building blocks that make up everything around us?
The center of an atom is called the _____.

questions.prompt: Match each particle to its description.

pairs.match: Positively charged particle
Neutral particle with no charge
Negatively charged particle that zips around the nucleus
The core of the atom

questions.front: What two particles are found in the nucleus of an atom?
questions.back: Protons and neutrons!

questions.question: What chart lists all the known elements in order?
The atomic number tells you how many _____ an atom has.

questions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning on the periodic table.

pairs.match: Number of protons in an element
Total protons plus neutrons
A vertical column on the periodic table
A horizontal row on the periodic table

questions.front: Where do electrons live in an atom?
questions.back: In a cloud that zips around the nucleus!

questions.question: What force holds the protons and neutrons tightly together in the nucleus?
The element symbol for lead is Pb, taken from its old Latin name '_____'.

questions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.match: Usually the first letter (or first two letters) of an element's name
The study of atoms and even tinier particles
The area around the nucleus where electrons zip around
Found by subtracting the atomic number from the mass number

questions.front: If an atom has an atomic number of 6 and a mass number of 12, how many neutrons does it have?
questions.back: 6 neutrons! (Mass number 12 minus atomic number 6 = 6 neutrons)

questions.question: What are atoms the building blocks of?
options: Molecules, which combine to make everything
Nothing -- they are the smallest thing that exists

questions.question: The Ancient Greek word 'atomos' means something that cannot be _____ any further.

questions.prompt: Match each particle to its charge.

pairs.match: Positive charge
No charge (neutral)
Negative charge
Made of protons and neutrons

questions.front: Why can't we actually see an individual atom, even with a microscope?
questions.back: Atoms are far too small -- there are no microscopes powerful enough yet, though scientists have other ways to detect and study them!

questions.question: What decides which chemical element an atom is?
The number of _____ in an atom defines its chemical properties, like melting and boiling point.

questions.prompt: Match each periodic table term to its meaning.

pairs.match: A vertical column of elements with similar properties
A horizontal row of elements on the table
The number of protons in an atom
The total number of protons and neutrons

questions.front: In a neutral atom, how does the number of electrons compare to the number of protons?
questions.back: They're equal! That's what keeps a neutral atom's overall charge balanced.

questions.question: How is an element's symbol usually written?
options: First letter capital, second letter (if any) lowercase

questions.question: The study of atoms and even tinier particles is called quantum _____.

questions.prompt: Match each element symbol to its element.

pairs.match: Carbon
Neon
Potassium
Lead

questions.front: Why is 'Co' different from 'CO' on the periodic table?
questions.back: 'Co' (capital-lowercase) is the single element cobalt, while 'CO' (two capitals) means two different elements -- carbon and oxygen -- combined!

questions.question: What can atoms be classified into, based on the article 'An Atom Apart'?
options: Elements, like oxygen, carbon, or hydrogen

questions.question: Carbon's element symbol is C, its atomic number is 6, and its mass number is _____.

questions.prompt: Match each part of an atom to where it's found.

pairs.match: In the nucleus
In the nucleus
In a cloud around the nucleus
The core, or center, of the atom

questions.front: What holds protons and neutrons so tightly together in the nucleus, even though protons repel each other?
questions.back: The electromagnetic force -- it's strong enough to overcome the protons' repulsion and keep the nucleus together!

questions.question: If you know an element's atomic number, what else can you figure out?
options: Its number of electrons (they're equal in a neutral atom)

questions.question: To find the number of neutrons in an atom, subtract the atomic number from the _____ number.

funFacts: The word 'atom' comes from the Greek 'atomos', meaning something that cannot be divided.
Atoms are so small that no microscope today is powerful enough to see one directly.
In a neutral atom, the number of electrons always equals the number of protons.
The periodic table arranges elements by atomic number, not by name.
Lead's symbol is Pb, from its old Latin name 'plumbum'.
The electromagnetic force is strong enough to hold protons together in the nucleus, even though they repel each other.
Quantum mechanics is the science of studying particles even tinier than atoms.
A vertical column on the periodic table is called a group; a horizontal row is called a period.

levels.name: Earth Rotation

questions.question: How long does it take Earth to complete one full rotation?
Earth's _____ causes day and night.

questions.prompt: Match each word to its meaning.

pairs.match: Earth spinning on its axis (24 hours)
Earth orbiting the Sun (365 days)
The imaginary line Earth spins around
Caused by Earth's tilt and revolution

questions.front: Why do we have seasons?
questions.back: Because Earth is tilted on its axis as it orbits the Sun, so different parts get more or less direct sunlight!

questions.question: How long does it take Earth to revolve once around the Sun?
The imaginary line Earth spins around is called its _____.

questions.prompt: Match each moon phase to its description.

pairs.match: Moon looks completely dark
Moon looks like a complete bright circle
A thin sliver that is growing bigger
Almost a full moon, but shrinking

questions.front: Does the Sun really move across the sky?
questions.back: No! It only looks that way because Earth is rotating.

questions.question: Which direction does the Sun appear to rise?
The curved path Earth takes around the Sun is called its _____.

questions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.match: Located above the North Pole -- it barely seems to move
An extra day added because a year is really 365 1/4 days
The curved path Earth takes around the Sun
West

questions.front: Why do stars and the Sun both seem to rise in the East and set in the West?
questions.back: Because Earth spins from West to East on its axis, making everything in the sky appear to move the opposite way!

questions.question: True or false: the side of Earth NOT reached by the Sun's rays experiences daytime.
Earth's movement through _____ affects life on Earth, including causing day and night.

questions.prompt: Match each moon phase to its description.

pairs.match: Half the moon is lit, right after waxing crescent
Half the moon is lit, after waning gibbous
A thin sliver that is shrinking, right before new moon
The moon looks completely dark

questions.front: Why does it get warmer or colder depending on the season?
questions.back: Because Earth is tilted on its axis -- when your part of the planet is closer to the Sun, you get summer; when it's farther away, you get winter!

questions.question: True or false: it takes Earth 369 days to orbit the Sun.
A year with one extra day added is called a _____ year.

questions.prompt: Match each term to its correct meaning.

pairs.match: Moving in a path around something, like Earth around the Sun
Spinning in place on an axis
An imaginary line through the North and South Poles
The curved path a planet takes around the Sun

questions.front: If you stand in place and spin around in a circle, what do you notice -- and how does this explain why the Sun seems to move across the sky?
questions.back: Objects appear to move around you, even though you're the one moving! The same thing happens with Earth -- it's rotating, not the Sun actually moving.

questions.question: Why do some groups of stars seem to circle around Polaris, the North Star, at night?
options: Because Earth rotates on its axis once each day

questions.question: Polaris doesn't seem to move much in the night sky because it is located above the _____ Pole.

questions.prompt: Match each statement to True or False.

pairs.term: There are four seasons: Spring, Summer, Autumn, and Winter
pairs.match: True

pairs.term: The occurrence of day and night is caused by Earth's rotation
pairs.match: True

pairs.term: Revolving means to move up and down
pairs.match: False

pairs.term: It takes the Sun 24 hours to rotate around Earth
pairs.match: False -- Earth rotates, not the Sun

questions.front: Why does the Moon seem to rise and set at different times and places in the sky, just like the Sun?
questions.back: Because the Moon is orbiting Earth while Earth spins -- the combination makes it appear to rise and set!

questions.question: What is the imaginary line that Earth spins around, running through the North and South Poles?
One full Earth _____ takes about 365 and a quarter days, which is why we add a leap day every 4 years.

questions.prompt: Match each cause to its effect.

pairs.match: Day and night
One year passing
The four seasons
The changing moon phases

questions.front: How long does one full Earth rotation take, and what does it give us?
questions.back: About 24 hours -- one full rotation gives us one day and one night!

questions.question: Earth's axis is an invisible line that intersects Earth through both the North and _____ Poles.
The different shapes the Moon appears to have in the sky are called moon _____.

funFacts: Earth rotates once every 24 hours -- that's one full day and night.
It takes Earth about 365 and a quarter days to orbit the Sun, which is why we add a leap day every 4 years.
Polaris, the North Star, barely seems to move because it sits almost directly above the North Pole.
The Moon goes through 8 different phases, from new moon to full moon and back again.
Seasons happen because Earth is tilted on its axis, not because Earth gets closer to or farther from the Sun.
The Sun doesn't actually move across the sky -- Earth's rotation just makes it look that way.
Earth spins from west to east, which is why the Sun appears to rise in the east.
Revolving means orbiting around something, like Earth around the Sun -- not moving up and down!

levels.name: Globes and Maps

questions.question: What is the imaginary line that divides Earth into Northern and Southern halves?
Lines that run east-west around the globe, like the equator, are called lines of _____.

questions.prompt: Match each term to what it means.

pairs.match: Divides Earth into North and South halves
Divides Earth into East and West halves
The very top of the Earth
Opposite side of the globe from the Prime Meridian

questions.front: What are lines of longitude used for?
questions.back: They run north-south, from pole to pole, and help measure how far east or west a place is!

questions.question: On a map, which direction is usually at the top?
A map's _____ explains what each symbol on the map means.

questions.prompt: Match each compass letter to its direction.

pairs.match: North
South
East
West

questions.front: What is a compass rose used for on a map?
questions.back: It shows the four main directions -- North, South, East, and West!

questions.question: About what fraction of Earth's surface is covered by land?
Because Earth has more water than land, it's nicknamed the '_____ Planet'.

questions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.match: A line north of the equator
A line south of the equator
The top half of the Earth
The bottom half of the Earth

questions.front: Why is Earth sometimes called the 'Blue Planet'?
questions.back: Because about three-quarters of its surface is covered by water -- rivers, seas, and oceans!

questions.question: Which planet is Earth, counting outward from the Sun?
The equator sits at 0 degrees _____.

questions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.match: Roughly on the opposite side of the globe from the Prime Meridian
The line of longitude at 0 degrees
The line of latitude at 0 degrees
One half of the Earth

questions.front: What continents does the equator pass through?
questions.back: Africa and South America!

questions.question: Which continent lies completely within the Southern Hemisphere?
A map's key or _____ explains what each symbol on the map represents.

questions.prompt: Match each direction to its position on a standard map.

pairs.match: Usually at the top
Usually at the bottom
Usually on the right
Usually on the left

questions.front: Why do maps use symbols and a key?
questions.back: Symbols make maps easier to read at a glance, and the key tells you exactly what each symbol means!

questions.question: In which ocean does the Prime Meridian cross the equator?
Longitude lines run from the North Pole to the South Pole and measure distance east and _____.

questions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.match: The very top of the Northern Hemisphere
The very bottom of the Southern Hemisphere
The dividing line between the two hemispheres
A map symbol showing N, S, E, and W

questions.front: What are the two main sets of imaginary lines used to locate any place on Earth's globe?
questions.back: Lines of latitude (running east-west) and lines of longitude (running north-south)!

questions.question: What does a compass rose show on a map?
options: The four main directions: North, South, East, West

questions.question: The imaginary line dividing Earth into Eastern and Western halves is called the Prime _____.

questions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.match: Lines running east-west, parallel to the equator
Lines running north-south, pole to pole
The half of Earth above the equator
The half of Earth below the equator

questions.front: Why is Earth sometimes described as looking like a 'ball covered with white clouds' from space?
questions.back: Because from space you can see swirling white clouds over the brown land and blue oceans below!

questions.question: About how much of Earth's surface has oxygen present on it?
Earth is often called the 'Blue Planet' and is the only known planet with _____.

funFacts: Earth is often called the 'Blue Planet' because about three-quarters of it is covered in water.
The equator is an imaginary line at 0 degrees latitude that circles the middle of the Earth.
The Prime Meridian sits at 0 degrees longitude and crosses the equator in the Atlantic Ocean.
Lines of latitude run east-west; lines of longitude run north-south, pole to pole.
A compass rose on a map shows the four main directions: North, South, East, and West.
The equator passes through both Africa and South America.
Australia is the only continent that lies completely within the Southern Hemisphere.
A map's key, or legend, tells you exactly what each symbol on the map means.

levels.name: Time System

questions.question: In the 24-hour clock, what time is 7:25 p.m.?
Noon means it is _____ o'clock in the daytime.

questions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.match: Time before noon
Time after noon
12:00 in the middle of the day
00:00 in the middle of the night

questions.front: How do you turn a p.m. time into 24-hour time?
questions.back: Add 12 to the hour! For example, 7:25 p.m. becomes 19:25.

questions.question: What time is midnight on the 24-hour clock?
A digital clock shows time using numbers, while an _____ clock uses moving hands.

questions.prompt: Match each 24-hour clock time to the correct a.m./p.m. time.

questions.front: Why do we use the 24-hour clock?
questions.back: It avoids mixing up a.m. and p.m. by counting all 24 hours in a day, from 00:00 to 23:59!

questions.question: Which is longer: 1 hour and 10 minutes, or 80 minutes?
Another word for 'noon', or 12:00 in the middle of the day, is _____.

questions.prompt: Match each time to its 24-hour clock equivalent.

questions.front: How many days are in the month of March?
questions.back: 31 days!

questions.question: What is 08:40 p.m. written as in 24-hour time?
14:15 in 24-hour time is the same as _____:15 p.m.

questions.prompt: Match each 12-hour time to its 24-hour clock equivalent.

questions.front: 'Seven minutes to eight at night' -- what time is that in 24-hour clock format?
questions.back: 19:53! Seven minutes to eight is 7:53, and 'at night' means p.m., so add 12 to get 19:53.

questions.question: If it is 10 weeks and 2 days until Christmas, how many days is that?
Your bus leaves at 9:30 a.m. and you must arrive 20 minutes early. What time must you arrive at school?

questions.prompt: Match each activity time to how long it takes.

pairs.match: 312 seconds
70 minutes
1 hour and 20 minutes
72 days

questions.front: You must be at school 20 minutes before the 9:30 a.m. bus, and it takes 15 minutes to walk from your house. What time should you leave home?
questions.back: 8:55 a.m.! You need to be at school by 9:10 a.m., and walking there takes 15 minutes, so leave at 8:55 a.m.

questions.question: A bike race starts at 11:30 a.m. Your friend crosses the finish line at 1:10 p.m. How long were they cycling for?
How many days are in the month of March? _____ days.

questions.prompt: Using the March 2020 calendar (March 1 is a Sunday), match each date to its day of the week.

pairs.match: Friday
Wednesday
Sunday
Tuesday

questions.front: How many Mondays are in March 2020, based on the calendar (March 1 is a Sunday)?
questions.back: 5 Mondays! They fall on March 2, 9, 16, 23, and 30.

questions.question: How many Fridays are in March 2020, based on the calendar (March 1 is a Sunday)?
An analogue clock shows time using moving _____, while a digital clock shows time using numbers.

questions.prompt: Match each clock time description to what it means.

pairs.match: Any time before noon
Any time after noon
12:00 in the middle of the day
00:00 in the middle of the night

questions.front: Why might someone prefer a lunch break of 80 minutes over 1 hour and 10 minutes?
questions.back: Because 80 minutes is actually longer! 1 hour 10 minutes is only 70 minutes, so 80 minutes gives you 10 extra minutes.

questions.question: To change a p.m. time into 24-hour time, what do you do?
10 weeks and 2 days is the same as 72 _____.

funFacts: The 24-hour clock avoids mixing up a.m. and p.m. by counting every hour of the day, from 00:00 to 23:59.
To change a p.m. time into 24-hour time, just add 12 to the hour.
Noon and midday both mean the same thing: 12:00 in the middle of the day.
Midnight is 00:00 on the 24-hour clock -- the very start of a new day.
1 hour and 10 minutes is actually 70 minutes, so 80 minutes is a bit longer!
March has 31 days, just like January, May, July, August, October, and December.
An analogue clock uses moving hands, while a digital clock displays time using numbers.
10 weeks and 2 days adds up to exactly 72 days.
